lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Mele steps back from both rooms — the harbour and the translation hall — and holds them together.

lines.speaker: Mele
lines.text: What strikes me is how similar the logic is. In Alexandria, Roman money kept the Mouseion alive and Roman fiscal pressure hollowed it out. In Baghdad, a caliph's political fear funded a century of translation.

lines.speaker: Mele
lines.text: The patron giveth and the patron taketh away. Sometimes in the same reign.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: That entanglement — ambition as both engine and liability — is not a footnote to these stories. It is the structure of them.

lines.speaker: Mele
lines.text: Every library we'll visit inherited this problem. Who pays for knowledge shapes what knowledge survives. And who pays can always stop paying.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: The question of access, then, is never just about the door. It begins with who built the door, and why they might one day close it.

lines.speaker: Mele
lines.text: That's what we'll keep asking. What survives. Who decided. And who was never meant to know.
